title: Adjuvant intravenous immunoglobulin in elderly sepsis: a randomized controlled study of mortality, organ function, and inflammation
one_line_summary: In this single-center RCT of 120 elderly sepsis patients, adjunctive IVIG (**0.4 g**/kg/day for 3 days) reduced 28-day **mortality** by 13.4% and accelerated SOFA score recovery compared to conventional therapy alone.
key_pearls: Adjunctive IVIG in elderly (≥65 years) sepsis patients was associated with a **13.4% absolute risk reduction** in 28-day **mortality** (18.3% vs 31.7%, RR 0.58, 95% CI 0.31–0.97, p=0.043), yielding an NNT of approximately **7**.
Organ function recovery was **significantly** accelerated: mean SOFA reduction at day 7 was **3.7 ± 1.2** points with IVIG vs **2.1 ± 1.0** points in controls (p < 0.001).

Systemic inflammation resolved more rapidly: CRP reduction at day 7 was **55.6 ± 10.4 mg/L** (IVIG) vs **38.3 ± 9.7 mg/L** (control), and procalcitonin reduction was **5.3 ± 1.6 ng/mL** vs **3.1 ± 1.4 ng/mL** (both p < 0.001).

ICU length of stay was shorter with IVIG (median **9.8 days** [IQR 8.0–12.0] vs **12.4 days** [10.0–15.0], p=0.024), as was mechanical ventilation duration (median **4.2 days** [3.5–5.0] vs **5.7 days** [4.5–7.0], p=0.011).

The **survival** benefit signal requires cautious interpretation: the log-rank test was non-**significant** (p=0.571), and the adjusted Cox model yielded HR **0.61** (95% CI 0.35–1.06, p=0.081), indicating the study was underpowered for time-to-event analysis.

IVIG demonstrated a favorable safety profile with no severe infusion reactions, thromboembolic events, or hemolysis; mild transient rash occurred in **5.0%** of the IVIG group vs 0% in controls (p=0.12).
population: Elderly patients (≥65 years) meeting Sepsis-3 criteria admitted to a single ICU in Hebei, China; mean age 73.6–74.1 years, balanced comorbidities including hypertension, diabetes, CKD, COPD.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content:
- Sepsis **mortality** in the elderly (≥65 years) often exceeds **25%** due to immunosenescence and high comorbidity burden.
- Conventional therapy (antimicrobials, organ support) does not adequately modulate the dysregulated host immune response central to sepsis pathophysiology.
- IVIG is a polyclonal IgG preparation with multimodal immunomodulatory effects: neutralization of bacterial exotoxins and superantigens, suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, modulation of complement activation and Fc-receptor function.
- Prior clinical evidence for IVIG in sepsis is inconsistent; efficacy signals appear more pronounced in subgroups with severe streptococcal or staphylococcal infections where toxin neutralization is crucial.
- The specific efficacy and safety of IVIG in the elderly septic population—a group with distinct immune biology and pharmacokinetics—has not been thoroughly investigated in a prospective randomized setting, creating a **significant** evidence gap.

sections.heading: Materials and Methods
sections.content:
- **Study Design**: Single-center, prospective, randomized, controlled, open-label with blinded outcome assessment; conducted at Cangzhou Central Hospital, Hebei, China (January 2023 – June 2024).
- **Ethics**: Approved by Institutional Review Board/Ethics Committee [Approval No: 2024-1201-01(z)]; written informed consent obtained; conducted per Declaration of Helsinki and Good Clinical Practice.
- **Participants**: 120 elderly patients (≥65 years) meeting Sepsis-3 criteria (suspected infection with acute SOFA increase ≥2 points) enrolled from 150 screened.
- **Key Exclusion Criteria**: Known hypersensitivity to immunoglobulin preparations; primary immunodeficiency disorders; active major hemorrhage or intracranial hemorrhage; DNR order or life expectancy <48h; prior IVIG within 3 months; IgA deficiency with known anti-IgA antibodies.
- **Intervention**: IVIG group received **0.4 g/kg/day** (Human Immunoglobulin pH4, Shanghai RAAS Blood Products) diluted in 5% glucose, infused over 6–8h for 3 consecutive days, starting within 24h of sepsis criteria, plus conventional therapy. Pre-medication with hydrocortisone 50–**100 mg** and/or chlorpheniramine given **30 min** before infusion.
- **Control**: Conventional therapy alone per Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines (early empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobials, fluid resuscitation, vasopressor support, organ-specific support).
- **Randomization and Blinding**: 1:1 computer-generated block randomization with sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes; outcome assessors and statistical analysts blinded.
- **Primary Outcomes**: 28-day all-cause **mortality**; change in SOFA score from baseline to day 7.
- **Secondary Outcomes**: Reduction in CRP and procalcitonin at days 3 and 7; duration of mechanical ventilation; ICU length of stay; adverse events graded by CTCAE v5.0.
- **Statistical Analysis**: Intention-to-treat; sample size calculated to detect **12% absolute risk reduction** (31% to 19%) with 80% power at two-sided alpha 0.05; linear mixed-effects model for repeated measures; Kaplan-Meier with log-rank test; Cox proportional hazards model adjusted for baseline SOFA, APACHE II, and age; no adjustment for multiple comparisons on secondary outcomes.
section_pearls: IVIG dosing was **0.4 g/kg/day for 3 consecutive days**, initiated within 24h of meeting sepsis criteria.
The study was powered to detect a **12% absolute mortality reduction** (from 31% to 19%) with 80% power.

sections.heading: Results
sections.content:
- **Patient Flow**: 150 patients screened, 120 randomized (60 IVIG, 60 control); baseline characteristics well balanced (mean age 73.6 vs 74.1 years, SOFA 8.3 vs 8.1, APACHE II 21.4 vs 20.9; all p > 0.05).
- **Primary Outcome – Mortality**: 28-day all-cause **mortality** was **18.3%** (11/60) in IVIG group vs **31.7%** (19/60) in controls (RR 0.58, 95% CI 0.31–0.97, p=0.043). Kaplan-Meier log-rank test non-**significant** (p=0.571). Adjusted Cox model HR **0.61** (95% CI 0.35–1.06, p=0.081).
- **Death Timing**: 71% of control deaths occurred within first 10 days; IVIG deaths evenly distributed across 28 days.
- **Primary Outcome – Organ Function**: SOFA reduction at day 7 was **3.7 ± 1.2** points (IVIG) vs **2.1 ± 1.0** points (control), p < 0.001; group-by-time interaction p < 0.001 in linear mixed-effects model.
- **Secondary – Inflammation**: CRP reduction at day 7: **55.6 ± 10.4 mg/L** vs **38.3 ± 9.7 mg/L** (p < 0.001). Procalcitonin reduction at day 7: **5.3 ± 1.6 ng/mL** vs **3.1 ± 1.4 ng/mL** (p < 0.001).
- **Secondary – Resource Utilization**: Median mechanical ventilation duration: **4.2 days** [IQR 3.5–5.0] vs **5.7 days** [4.5–7.0] (p=0.011). Median ICU stay: **9.8 days** [8.0–12.0] vs **12.4 days** [10.0–15.0] (p=0.024).
- **Safety**: Adverse events infrequent and comparable (8.3% vs 6.7%, p=0.73). Mild transient rash in 3 patients (5.0%) in IVIG group vs 0 in controls (p=0.12). No thromboembolic events, hemolysis, or severe infusion reactions. Renal replacement therapy: 6.7% vs 10.0% (p=0.49).
- **Exploratory Subgroups**: Consistent SOFA reduction favoring IVIG across all infection sources (respiratory, urinary, intra-abdominal, skin/soft tissue) and pathogen types (Gram-negative, Gram-positive, polymicrobial), with differences ranging from +1.3 to +1.6 points. **Mortality** numerically lower in most subgroups except intra-abdominal and polymicrobial infections.
section_pearls: The absolute risk reduction in 28-day mortality was **13.4%** (NNT ≈ 7), but the log-rank test was non-significant (p=0.571), and the adjusted Cox model showed only a trend (HR 0.61, p=0.081).
SOFA reduction at day 7 favored IVIG by **1.6 points** (3.7 vs 2.1, p < 0.001).

sections.heading: Discussion
sections.content:
- The observed **13.4% absolute mortality reduction** (NNT ≈ 7) aligns with the immunomodulatory rationale for IVIG in elderly sepsis, where immunosenescence impairs pathogen clearance and amplifies maladaptive inflammation.
- Accelerated SOFA decline and more rapid CRP/procalcitonin reduction support a biological effect consistent with toxin neutralization, Fc-receptor modulation, and cytokine attenuation.
- The benefit may be particularly pronounced in the elderly due to distinct immune pathophysiology; this subgroup may represent a target population most likely to benefit from adjunctive immunotherapy.
- Reduction in ICU stay and mechanical ventilation duration has **significant** health economic implications, suggesting faster clinical stabilization and potential cost savings.
- The safety profile was favorable: no severe reactions, thromboembolic events, or hemolysis; low incidence of mild infusion reactions (5.0% rash) consistent with pharmacovigilance data.
- Discrepancy between chi-square (p=0.043) and log-rank (p=0.571) tests explained by death timing: 71% of control deaths occurred early (within 10 days), while IVIG deaths were evenly distributed; the log-rank test accounts for entire timeline and is influenced by later curve convergence; with only 30 total events, the study is underpowered for time-to-event analysis (>200 events needed for 80% power).
- Exploratory subgroup analyses showed consistent SOFA reduction favoring IVIG across all infection sources and pathogen types, supporting biological plausibility.

sections.heading: Strengths and Limitations
sections.content:
- **Strengths**: Prospective, randomized design; rigorous phenotyping of a specifically elderly sepsis cohort; use of validated organ dysfunction scores (SOFA); serial biomarker measurements supporting mechanistic link between immunomodulation and clinical improvement.
- **Limitations**: Single-center design limits generalizability; sample size (120 patients) may be insufficient for robust **mortality** conclusions—chi-square p-value (0.043) only marginally crossed significance, log-rank test non-**significant** (p=0.571), and lower bound of RR CI (0.31) approaches null effect; open-label design carries risk of performance bias despite blinded outcome assessment; follow-up restricted to 28 days, precluding evaluation of 90-day **mortality**, long-term functional status, and quality of life; subgroup analyses not powered for statistical comparisons and are hypothesis-generating only; no adjustment for multiple comparisons on secondary outcomes.
section_pearls: The study was underpowered for time-to-event analysis: only **30 total events** occurred, whereas >200 events are needed for 80% power in survival analysis.
The open-label design, despite blinded outcome assessment, carries a risk of performance bias.

sections.heading: Conclusion
sections.content:
- In elderly patients with sepsis, adjunctive IVIG was associated with reduced 28-day **mortality**, accelerated recovery of organ dysfunction, and more rapid resolution of systemic inflammation compared to conventional therapy alone.
- IVIG also reduced ICU resource utilization and demonstrated a favorable safety profile.
- While a statistically **significant** reduction in 28-day **mortality** was observed by chi-square test, the **survival** curve analysis showed a non-**significant** trend, highlighting the need for confirmatory studies.
- Validation in larger, multicenter studies is necessary to definitively establish efficacy, refine patient selection, and inform integration into standard sepsis management protocols for the elderly.

strengths_limitations:
- **Strengths**: Prospective randomized design with blinded outcome assessment; specific focus on elderly sepsis patients with distinct immune biology; serial biomarker measurements (CRP, procalcitonin) providing mechanistic support; use of validated organ dysfunction scores (SOFA).
- **Limitations**: Single-center design limits external generalizability; sample size (n=120) calculated for 12% **mortality** reduction but underpowered for time-to-event analysis (only 30 total events, log-rank p=0.571, adjusted Cox HR 0.61 with p=0.081); open-label design despite blinded assessment introduces potential performance bias; 28-day follow-up precludes evaluation of 90-day **mortality** and long-term functional outcomes; secondary outcomes not adjusted for multiple comparisons; subgroup analyses purely descriptive and hypothesis-generating.
